note: Benchmark costs and effort levels are directional estimates based on public leaderboards and API pricing. Replace with your own vendor quotes for exact budgeting.

coding.name: Coding agents
coding.description: Agents that write, edit, debug, review, or test code against real or synthetic repositories.

reasoning.name: Reasoning & knowledge
reasoning.description: Multi-step reasoning, tool use, world knowledge, and instruction following under time pressure.

web.name: Web agents
web.description: Agents that navigate websites, fill forms, search, and complete tasks in live or simulated browsers.

desktop.name: Desktop / OS agents
desktop.description: Agents that operate an operating system GUI: files, apps, settings, and multi-step productivity tasks.

multi-agent.name: Multi-agent systems
multi-agent.description: Teams of agents that communicate, delegate, negotiate, or compete to solve a shared problem.

domain.name: Domain-specific agents
domain.description: Specialized tasks such as math, cybersecurity, science, law, finance, or biomedicine.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench
benchmarks.tests: Real-world GitHub issues from popular Python repositories. The agent must edit code so the repository's own test suite passes.
benchmarks.pros: Highly realistic; directly measures production software engineering ability.
benchmarks.cons: Expensive to run; requires sandboxed execution and repo setup; not all issues are agent-friendly.
benchmarks.fit: Code-generation agents, AI software engineers, and autonomous PR bots.

benchmarks.name: HumanEval
benchmarks.tests: 164 hand-written Python function-completion problems with unit-test validation.
benchmarks.pros: Cheap, fast, and widely cited; great for comparing pass@1 coding skill across models.
benchmarks.cons: Short functions only; may be memorized by large models; does not test repo-level work.
benchmarks.fit: Quick model comparisons for code completion and small-function generation.

benchmarks.name: MBPP (Mostly Basic Python Problems)
benchmarks.tests: Crowd-sourced Python programming problems with test cases, from beginner to intermediate difficulty.
benchmarks.pros: Larger and more varied than HumanEval; includes docstrings and natural-language prompts.
benchmarks.cons: Still focused on small, self-contained functions; less realistic than repo-level benchmarks.
benchmarks.fit: Entry-level coding assistants and educational coding agents.

benchmarks.name: GAIA
benchmarks.tests: Multi-step question answering that requires reasoning, web browsing, file parsing, and tool use to answer real-world questions.
benchmarks.pros: Very hard to game; rewards agents that can plan, search, and synthesize evidence.
benchmarks.cons: Slow and expensive; requires web access; answers can be brittle to search-result changes.
benchmarks.fit: General-purpose assistant agents, research agents, and agents with tool access.

benchmarks.name: MMLU
benchmarks.tests: 57 multiple-choice tasks covering STEM, humanities, social sciences, and professional knowledge.
benchmarks.pros: Standardized, cheap, and widely reported; good baseline for world knowledge.
benchmarks.cons: Static multiple-choice benchmark; does not measure multi-step reasoning or agentic behavior.
benchmarks.fit: Foundation-model selection and knowledge-heavy assistant agents.

benchmarks.name: LiveBench
benchmarks.tests: Continuously updated reasoning tasks drawn from recent data sources, designed to avoid contamination.
benchmarks.pros: Harder to memorize; stays current; covers math, reasoning, language, and instruction following.
benchmarks.cons: Less established than MMLU or HumanEval; questions can be noisy.
benchmarks.fit: Agents that must stay current or claim strong general reasoning.

benchmarks.name: WebArena
benchmarks.tests: Independent, realistic web tasks across e-commerce, social forums, software development, maps, and admin panels.
benchmarks.pros: Simulates real websites; measures end-to-end task completion in a live environment.
benchmarks.cons: Requires hosted environments; can be flaky due to website changes; expensive to run at scale.
benchmarks.fit: Browser agents, web assistants, and autonomous research agents.

benchmarks.name: WebVoyager
benchmarks.tests: Visual web navigation tasks where the agent must interact with live websites through screenshots and predicted actions.
benchmarks.pros: Tests vision + web interaction; closer to how humans browse visually.
benchmarks.cons: Latency is high; requires screenshot rendering; brittle to UI redesigns.
benchmarks.fit: Vision-enabled browser agents and multimodal assistants.

benchmarks.name: Mind2Web
benchmarks.tests: Diverse web tasks on real websites across multiple domains, annotated with expert demonstrations.
benchmarks.pros: Large coverage of web domains; includes both action prediction and task completion metrics.
benchmarks.cons: Benchmark is static; newer website versions may diverge from the dataset.
benchmarks.fit: Generalist web agents and UI automation tools.

benchmarks.name: OSWorld
benchmarks.tests: Open-ended tasks inside a real Ubuntu desktop: file management, browser use, office apps, and system configuration.
benchmarks.pros: Real OS environment; captures messy, long-horizon computer use.
benchmarks.cons: Very expensive and slow; needs VM infrastructure; high variance across runs.
benchmarks.fit: Desktop automation agents, personal assistants, and OS-control agents.

benchmarks.name: Windows Agent Arena
benchmarks.tests: Tasks on a live Windows desktop spanning settings, apps, and cross-application workflows.
benchmarks.pros: Tests the most common consumer OS; surfaces enterprise automation value.
benchmarks.cons: Windows-specific licensing and VM setup; evaluation pipeline is complex.
benchmarks.fit: Enterprise desktop-automation agents and Windows-centric copilots.

benchmarks.name: AgentBench
benchmarks.tests: Eight environments (OS, database, knowledge graph, digital card game, web shopping, household, etc.) that exercise decision making, tool use, and multi-turn interaction.
benchmarks.pros: Broad multi-environment coverage; compares agents across many dimensions.
benchmarks.cons: Some environments are synthetic; setup can be heavy; scoring is nuanced.
benchmarks.fit: Generalist agents, tool-use agents, and multi-environment systems.

benchmarks.name: BenchMAX
benchmarks.tests: Multi-agent collaboration tasks such as negotiation, team problem solving, and resource allocation.
benchmarks.pros: Targets the unique failure modes of multi-agent communication and coordination.
benchmarks.cons: Smaller community; less standardized than single-agent benchmarks.
benchmarks.fit: Multi-agent orchestrators, agent teams, and collaborative AI systems.

benchmarks.name: GSM8K
benchmarks.tests: Grade-school math word problems that require multi-step arithmetic reasoning.
benchmarks.pros: Clean, cheap, and widely used; strong signal for chain-of-thought reasoning.
benchmarks.cons: Narrow domain; not representative of real agent deployments.
benchmarks.fit: Tutoring agents, math copilots, and reasoning-heavy assistants.

benchmarks.name: CYBER / CTF Evals
benchmarks.tests: Capture-the-flag and cybersecurity challenges ranging from reconnaissance to exploit development.
benchmarks.pros: Directly measures offensive-security and vulnerability-research capability.
benchmarks.cons: High stakes; requires isolated sandbox; not suitable for public leaderboards without review.
benchmarks.fit: Security research agents, red-team tools, and vulnerability-assessment copilots.

benchmarks.name: SciWorld
benchmarks.tests: Open-ended science experiments in a text-based simulation where agents must design and execute experiments.
benchmarks.pros: Tests scientific reasoning and long-horizon planning in a controlled simulator.
benchmarks.cons: Niche; requires domain expertise to interpret results; simulator limitations affect generalization.
benchmarks.fit: Scientific research assistants, lab-automation agents, and hypothesis-testing systems.
